# Ericsson — Public Overview

Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (publ), commonly known as Ericsson, is a Swedish multinational telecommunications networking and ICT company founded in 1876 and headquartered in Kista, Stockholm. Its shares are listed on Nasdaq Stockholm (ERIC-A, ERIC-B) and on Nasdaq in New York (ERIC). A dual-class share structure concentrates voting control in long-term Swedish owners, principally Investor AB (the Wallenberg sphere) and Industrivärden. The company employs roughly 89,000 people, down from a peak above 105,000 in 2022 after several years of cost reduction.

Ericsson is one of the world's leading suppliers of mobile network infrastructure and is consistently the number two radio access network (RAN) vendor globally, behind Huawei and ahead of Nokia, Samsung, and ZTE. It supplies hardware, software, and services to mobile operators worldwide, powering on the order of 170 live 5G networks across more than 70 countries.

## Financial profile (FY2025)

- Net sales of about SEK 237 billion, broadly flat organically (+2%) in a RAN market that the company and analysts describe as "flattish."
- A margin-led recovery from a weak 2022 to 2024 period: adjusted EBITA margin of about 18%, net income of about SEK 29 billion (up from near zero in 2024), and a net cash position of about SEK 61 billion.
- Recurring intellectual-property licensing revenue of about SEK 14.5 billion from a portfolio of more than 60,000 granted patents, with Ericsson a top holder of 5G standard-essential patents.
- A proposed dividend of SEK 3.00 per share and a SEK 15 billion share buyback.
- The recovery rests on cost discipline, favourable mix, and IPR rather than market growth. The United States is the single largest market at roughly a third of sales, which makes results sensitive to a few large operators.

## Business areas

- **Networks.** The largest segment, around two-thirds of sales. Ericsson Radio System, Massive MIMO radios, Cloud RAN, transport, and in-house Ericsson Silicon ASICs.
- **Cloud Software and Services.** 5G Core and packet core, signalling and routing, BSS and OSS, and managed services.
- **Enterprise.** Global communications platform and network APIs (including Vonage), and enterprise wireless solutions (Cradlepoint).
- **Other, including IPR.** Patent licensing and emerging adjacencies.

## Strategy and technology

Ericsson's publicly stated priorities are 5G and mobile-network leadership, expansion into enterprise and programmable networks, network-API monetisation through the Aduna joint venture (a 50:50 venture with a consortium of major operators, selling APIs on the GSMA CAMARA standard), AI in the RAN, and cost discipline toward a 15 to 18% EBITA margin target. The anchor commercial event of the period is the roughly USD 14 billion, five-year AT&T contract signed in late 2023, the largest in Ericsson's history, which expanded its Open RAN and Cloud RAN footprint. Ericsson is a founding member of the AI-RAN Alliance and has demonstrated Cloud RAN running on NVIDIA AI infrastructure, alongside its own purpose-built silicon.

## Leadership

President and CEO Börje Ekholm (since 2017), CFO Lars Sandström (since 2024), and Group CTO Erik Ekudden (since 2017). The board is chaired by Jan Carlson. In 2025 Ericsson consolidated its market areas into Americas and Europe, Middle East and Africa, alongside South East Asia, Oceania and India.

## Market position and competition

Ericsson differentiates on radio performance, end-to-end integration, software and AI-driven operations, IPR, and long-standing operator relationships. Its principal competitors are Nokia (comparable portfolio) and Huawei (broader portfolio, with market access shaped by geopolitics), with Samsung, ZTE, and Open RAN challengers in selected segments. Ericsson is treated as a trusted, non-high-risk vendor in Western markets, which creates replacement opportunities as some markets remove Chinese vendors, but it is effectively excluded from China. In the Gulf and wider MENA region it is an established supplier to leading operators across radio, core, and managed services. The principal risks to the business are a structurally flat RAN capex cycle, dependence on a few large US operators, currency, rising semiconductor input costs, and the China exclusion.

---

*Illustrative public-information summary for the MVP, drawn from public reporting as of mid-2026. Figures are rounded and approximate. Verify against current public sources before external use.*
